question: What is the official website for residence permit applications in Turkey?
answer: The only official website is the e-ikamet portal operated by the Directorate General of Migration Management. You must apply here: https://e-ikamet.goc.gov.tr/

question: How long can I stay in Turkey without a residence permit?
answer: Most nationalities can stay for up to 90 days within a 180-day period under visa exemption or an e-Visa. You must check your specific country's visa rules on the Ministry of Foreign Affairs website: https://www.mfa.gov.tr/visa-information-for-foreigners.en.mfa

question: When should I apply for my first residence permit?
answer: You must submit your online application through the official e-ikamet system before your visa or visa exemption period expires. Apply at: https://e-ikamet.goc.gov.tr/

question: What happens if I overstay my visa before applying?
answer: If you apply after your visa expires, your application will likely be rejected. You will also have to pay an overstay fine when leaving Turkey and may face a re-entry ban.

question: Can I leave Turkey while my residence permit application is pending?
answer: Yes, but you must obtain an application document (İkamet İzni Müracaat Belgesi) stamped by the migration office. With this, you can leave for up to 15 days without penalty.

question: What is a short-term residence permit?
answer: It is a permit typically issued for 1 to 2 years for purposes like tourism, business meetings, medical treatment, or learning Turkish. Currently, tourist permits are strictly regulated.

question: What documents do I need for a tourist residence permit?
answer: Standard requirements include a passport copy, 4 biometric photos, proof of sufficient funds, a notarized rental contract, valid health insurance, and a tax number. Full details are listed on: https://en.goc.gov.tr/

question: How do I prove my address for a residence permit?
answer: If you are renting, your rental agreement must be notarized in Turkey. The landlord's ID and title deed (tapu) are also usually required. You must register this address via the Nüfus Directorate or e-Devlet: https://www.turkiye.gov.tr/

question: Are there restricted neighborhoods for residence permits?
answer: Yes. To prevent demographic concentration, the government has closed over 1,000 neighborhoods across Turkey to new foreign registrations. You cannot get a first-time permit by renting in these areas. Announcements are made via: https://www.goc.gov.tr/

question: How much does a residence permit cost?
answer: Costs include a card fee, a visa fee (if applicable), health insurance, notary fees, and a residence permit tax which varies depending on your nationality.

question: How can I pay my residence permit tax?
answer: You can pay it online via the Interactive Tax Office (İnteraktif Vergi Dairesi) using your application number, at state banks, or directly at tax offices. Official tax portal: https://ivd.gib.gov.tr/

question: Can I work on a tourist residence permit?
answer: No. A residence permit does not give you the right to work in Turkey. Working without a specific work permit is illegal. Work permits are handled by the Ministry of Labor: https://www.csgb.gov.tr/

question: Who is eligible for a student residence permit?
answer: Foreigners enrolled in an associate, undergraduate, master's, or doctoral program at a Turkish university can apply for a student residence permit.

question: Does a language course grant a student residence permit?
answer: If the course is provided by a university (like TÖMER), you may get a short-term permit for the duration of the course. Private language schools usually do not qualify you for a student permit.

question: What is a family residence permit?
answer: It is granted to the foreign spouse, minor children, or dependent foreign children of a Turkish citizen or a foreigner legally residing in Turkey (the sponsor).

question: What income does a sponsor need for a family permit?
answer: The sponsor must have a monthly income not less than the minimum wage in total, and not less than one-third of the minimum wage per family member.

question: Who can apply for a long-term residence permit?
answer: Foreigners who have legally and continuously resided in Turkey for at least 8 years, have not received state aid recently, and have a stable income can apply.

question: Does a student permit count towards the 8 years for a long-term permit?
answer: Only half of the duration spent on a student residence permit counts towards the 8-year requirement.

question: How can I get a residence permit by buying property?
answer: You can get a short-term real estate residence permit if you buy a residential property. The property's minimum value must meet the current government threshold. Verification is done via the Land Registry: https://www.tkgm.gov.tr/

question: Do I need an appraisal report to buy property for a residence permit?
answer: Yes, an official real estate valuation report (ekspertiz raporu) is mandatory to prove the property meets the minimum financial threshold.

question: What happens after I submit my application online?
answer: You will be given an appointment date and time. You must go to the Provincial Directorate of Migration Management with your physical document file on that exact date.

question: Can someone else go to the migration appointment for me?
answer: No. You must attend the appointment in person because they need to collect your biometric data (fingerprints) and verify your identity.

question: What if my residence permit application is rejected?
answer: If rejected, you have 10 days to leave the country after receiving official notification. You can appeal the decision in court, but it is highly recommended to hire an immigration lawyer.

question: If rejected, when can I apply again?
answer: If rejected, you cannot apply for the same type of residence permit for 6 months. You can apply for a different type if you meet the requirements.

question: How long does it take for my ID card to arrive?
answer: After your appointment is approved, your residence permit card is printed in Ankara and sent to your registered address via PTT (national post) usually within 2 to 4 weeks. You can track PTT shipments here: https://gonderitakip.ptt.gov.tr/

question: What should I do if my address changes?
answer: You must notify the Provincial Directorate of Migration Management and the Civil Registry Office (Nüfus) within 20 working days of moving to a new address. Address changes can also be initiated via e-Devlet: https://www.turkiye.gov.tr/

question: Do I need to update my permit if I get a new passport?
answer: Yes. If you renew your passport, you must inform the migration office within 20 working days to update your file.

question: Can I renew my permit online without going to the office?
answer: Historically yes, but under recent rules, you are often required to attend an appointment in person even for renewals or mail your documents via PTT depending on your province's specific instructions.

question: When should I apply for renewal?
answer: You can start your renewal application up to 60 days before your current permit expires. You must apply before the expiration date via https://e-ikamet.goc.gov.tr/

question: What is an apostille and do I need it?
answer: An apostille is an international certification. Documents like birth certificates, marriage certificates, or criminal records brought from abroad must be apostilled or legalized by a Turkish consulate to be valid in Turkey.

question: How do I get my foreign documents translated?
answer: Foreign documents must be translated into Turkish by a sworn translator (yeminli tercüman) in Turkey and then certified by a Turkish Notary Public.

question: Is a criminal record check required?
answer: It is not always required for short-term tourist permits, but it is mandatory for long-term permits and citizenship applications. The migration office may request it at their discretion.

question: Do babies and children need their own applications?
answer: Yes, every individual, including infants, must have their own separate application form and pay the relevant fees.

question: Can I track my application status?
answer: Yes, you can track it on the e-ikamet website (https://e-ikamet.goc.gov.tr/) using your application number or by calling the YİMER 157 helpline.

question: What is YİMER 157?
answer: YİMER 157 is the official communication center for foreigners in Turkey. You can call them 24/7 for help with visas, residence permits, and emergencies. Website: https://yimer.gov.tr/

question: What is a humanitarian residence permit?
answer: It is an exceptional permit granted to foreigners suffering from extraordinary circumstances, such as civil war in their home country or severe medical conditions preventing travel.

question: What happens to my permit if I stay out of Turkey too long?
answer: Under current laws, short-term and student residence permits are NOT canceled if you stay abroad for more than 120 days. However, extended absences can affect your eligibility for long-term residency or citizenship.

question: Can my permit be canceled?
answer: Yes. It can be canceled if it is found to be used for purposes other than intended, if you are deported, or if you present forged documents.

question: Does my passport validity affect my permit duration?
answer: Yes. Your residence permit will be issued to expire at least 60 days before your passport expiration date. Make sure your passport has plenty of validity.

question: What is the UETS system?
answer: UETS (National Electronic Notification System) is an electronic mailing address. Foreigners applying for residence permits are now required to obtain a UETS address to receive official notifications. Learn more: https://www.etebligat.gov.tr/

question: I lost my residence permit card. What should I do?
answer: You must immediately report it to the nearest police station to get a lost item report. Then, apply to the migration office with a new petition, photos, and the police report to have it reprinted (requires paying the card fee again).

question: Does buying a car give me a residence permit?
answer: No. Purchasing a vehicle does not provide any grounds for applying for or obtaining a residence permit in Turkey.

question: What proof of income is needed for a tourist permit?
answer: Migration officers typically require a Turkish bank statement showing regular deposits or a substantial balance to prove you can support yourself without working (often calculated as 1.5 times the minimum wage per month).

question: Can a friend sponsor my tourist residence permit?
answer: A Turkish citizen can sign an official letter of undertaking (Taahhütname) at a notary, taking full financial and legal responsibility for your stay. However, approval is strictly at the migration office's discretion.

question: Do I need a biometric photo?
answer: Yes. The photo must have been taken within the last 6 months against a white background, following ICAO standards. Do not edit or filter the photo.

question: Can I use travel insurance for my application?
answer: No. Standard tourist travel insurance is not accepted. You must purchase specific 'Foreigner Health Insurance' (Yabancı Sağlık Sigortası) from a Turkish insurance company.

question: What happens if I marry a Turkish citizen?
answer: You can apply for a family residence permit. After 3 years of marriage, you may become eligible to apply for Turkish citizenship, provided the marriage is genuine and certain conditions are met.

question: I have dual citizenship. Which passport should I use?
answer: You must apply for the residence permit using the passport you used to enter Turkey. It is not possible to enter with one passport and apply with another.

question: Can a lawyer guarantee my residence permit approval?
answer: No. While a lawyer can ensure your paperwork is flawless, the final decision belongs entirely to the Directorate General of Migration Management. Beware of agencies promising '100% guaranteed' permits.

question: What is the penalty for overstaying?
answer: The penalty includes an administrative fine calculated based on your nationality and days overstayed. Refusing to pay at the airport will result in an immediate 5-year entry ban.

question: What are the best websites to find an apartment for rent in Turkey?
answer: The most popular real estate websites are Sahibinden (https://www.sahibinden.com), Hepsiemlak (https://www.hepsiemlak.com), and Zingat. You can filter by city, district, and property features.

question: What is an 'Emlakçı'?
answer: An 'Emlakçı' is a real estate agent. In Turkey, the vast majority of rental properties are managed by real estate agents rather than directly by the owners.

question: How much is the real estate agent commission?
answer: By law, the real estate agent commission (emlakçı komisyonu) is equal to one month's rent plus 20% VAT. This fee is paid entirely by the tenant.

question: How much deposit do landlords usually ask for?
answer: Landlords typically ask for a security deposit (depozito) equivalent to one or two months' rent. Under the Turkish Code of Obligations, it cannot legally exceed three months' rent.

question: What does 'Aidat' mean?
answer: Aidat is the monthly building maintenance fee. It covers shared expenses like staircase lighting, cleaning, elevator maintenance, and, in complexes, pool and security costs.

question: What does 'Eşyalı' mean when looking for apartments?
answer: Eşyalı means the apartment is fully furnished. It usually includes furniture, white goods (fridge, washing machine), and sometimes even basic kitchenware.

question: If an apartment is 'Eşyasız' (unfurnished), what does it include?
answer: An unfurnished apartment in Turkey is completely empty. It usually does not even include an oven, stove, refrigerator, or washing machine. You only get the kitchen cabinets and bathroom fixtures.

question: Do I need a residence permit to rent a house?
answer: No, you can sign a rental contract using just your passport. However, you will need a residence permit or at least a tax number to register the utility bills in your name via e-Devlet (https://www.turkiye.gov.tr/).

question: Why do I need to notarize my rental contract?
answer: If you are applying for a residence permit, the migration office requires your rental contract to be notarized in the presence of both you and the landlord. You can find the nearest notary at: https://portal.tnb.org.tr/

question: Who pays the notary fee for the rental contract?
answer: By general custom in Turkey, the tenant pays the notary fees associated with legalizing the rental contract for residence permit purposes.

question: How long is a standard rental contract?
answer: Standard rental contracts are for 1 year. Under Turkish law, the contract automatically renews at the end of the year unless the tenant gives formal notice to leave.

question: Can my landlord kick me out before my 1-year contract ends?
answer: No. A landlord cannot evict you before the contract ends unless you stop paying rent or severely damage the property, which requires a formal court process.

question: How much can the landlord increase my rent after one year?
answer: Rent increases are legally capped based on the 12-month average of the Consumer Price Index (TÜFE), announced monthly by the Turkish Statistical Institute: https://www.tuik.gov.tr/

question: How should I pay my rent?
answer: Rent should always be paid via bank transfer to the landlord's account. You must write 'Kira Bedeli' (Rent Payment) and the month in the transfer description.

question: Can I pay my rent in cash?
answer: It is highly discouraged and illegal for amounts over a certain threshold. Paying in cash leaves you with no legal proof of payment if the landlord claims you didn't pay.

question: What is DASK?
answer: DASK is the mandatory national earthquake insurance. You need a valid DASK policy document to open electricity, water, and gas subscriptions in your name. Verify policies at: https://dask.gov.tr/

question: Who pays for the DASK insurance?
answer: Legally, the landlord must pay for and renew the DASK insurance. However, they will give you the policy number so you can register your utilities.

question: How do I put the electricity and water in my name?
answer: You need to visit the local utility providers or apply via e-Devlet (https://www.turkiye.gov.tr/) with your rental contract, passport/kimlik, tax number, and DASK policy.

question: Do I have to pay a deposit for utilities?
answer: Yes, utility companies charge a one-time security deposit (güvence bedeli) when opening the subscription. It is added to your first bill or paid upfront.

question: Will I get my utility deposits back?
answer: Yes. When you move out and close the accounts, the utility companies will refund your deposits (adjusted for inflation) to your bank account within a few days.

question: What is a 'Kefil'?
answer: A 'Kefil' is a guarantor. It is a person who signs the contract with you and legally promises to pay the rent if you fail to do so.

question: Do landlords require a guarantor for foreigners?
answer: Yes, it is very common. Many landlords ask for a Turkish citizen with a steady income to act as a guarantor for foreign tenants.

question: I don't have a Turkish guarantor. What can I do?
answer: If you cannot provide a guarantor, landlords often ask for 6 months or even 1 year of rent paid upfront in cash or transfer to secure the contract.

question: Can I sublet my apartment or a room to someone else?
answer: Usually no. Standard Turkish rental contracts strictly forbid subletting (alt kiralama) without the explicit written permission of the landlord.

question: Are pets allowed in rental apartments?
answer: You must ask the landlord and check the contract. Some building management rules (yönetim planı) also explicitly ban pets, which can lead to eviction.

question: What is a 'Site'?
answer: A 'Site' is a residential housing complex. It usually features multiple buildings enclosed by walls, offering amenities like 24/7 security, parking, gyms, and swimming pools.

question: Why is the aidat in a 'Site' so expensive?
answer: Because it covers the salaries of the security guards, gardeners, and cleaning staff, as well as the maintenance of luxury facilities like pools and fitness centers.

question: What is a 'Kombi'?
answer: A Kombi is an individual gas boiler installed in your apartment. It provides both central heating (via radiators) and hot water. You only pay for the gas you use.

question: What is 'Merkezi Sistem' heating?
answer: Central heating means the whole building is heated by a single massive boiler. The monthly gas bill is divided among the apartments, usually based on their square meter size.

question: What is a 'Pay Ölçer'?
answer: It is a heat cost allocator used in central heating systems. It measures exactly how much heat your specific radiators emit, so you pay based on your actual usage rather than a flat rate.

question: How do I hire someone to move my furniture?
answer: You can search for 'Evden Eve Nakliyat' (door-to-door moving companies) online or ask your real estate agent. They will pack, transport, and assemble your furniture.

question: How can I avoid rental scams?
answer: Never send money or a deposit via wire transfer before physically seeing the apartment, meeting the agent/landlord, and signing a formal contract.

question: Why did the real estate agent say the apartment online is 'just rented'?
answer: This is a common bait-and-switch tactic. They post fake, beautiful, cheap apartments to get you to call, then say it's taken and try to show you more expensive ones.

question: What does 'Sahibinden' mean on property listings?
answer: It means the property is listed directly 'from the owner'. Renting sahibinden means you will not have to pay the 1-month real estate agent commission.

question: What is a 'Demirbaş Listesi'?
answer: It is the inventory list attached to the rental contract. It records the fixtures and items provided (e.g., boiler, keys, air conditioner) and their current condition.

question: Will I be charged for normal wear and tear?
answer: Under Turkish law, tenants are not responsible for normal wear and tear caused by regular usage over time. You are only liable for damages caused by misuse or negligence.

question: How do I get my deposit back?
answer: When moving out, the landlord will inspect the apartment. If there are no unpaid bills or damages beyond normal wear and tear, they must return the deposit to your bank account.

question: How much notice do I need to give before moving out?
answer: You must send a formal written notice (preferably via a notary) at least 15 days before the annual contract renewal date stating you will not renew.

question: What happens if I break my lease early?
answer: If you leave before the contract ends, you are legally responsible for paying rent until the landlord finds a new tenant (usually calculated by courts as a maximum of 2-3 months' rent).

question: What is a 'Tahliye Taahhütnamesi'?
answer: It is an 'eviction commitment' document. Landlords often force tenants to sign this, which states you promise to vacate the apartment on a specific date.

question: Should I sign a 'Tahliye Taahhütnamesi' with a blank date?
answer: No! If you sign it without a date, the landlord can fill in any date they want in the future and legally evict you within a month without a court trial.

question: Can the landlord demand rent in US Dollars or Euros?
answer: Generally, it is illegal to price rent in foreign currency for residents in Turkey. Rent must be agreed upon and paid in Turkish Lira (TRY), with some rare exceptions for foreign companies.

question: Does the landlord have the right to keep a spare key?
answer: No. Once you rent the apartment, it is your private residence. The landlord cannot keep a key or enter the apartment without your explicit permission.

question: Am I allowed to change the locks?
answer: Yes, it is highly recommended to change the lock cylinder (göbek) as soon as you move in for your own security. You do not need to give the new key to the landlord.

question: Do I have to paint the apartment when I move out?
answer: If the contract states you received the apartment freshly painted ('boyalı teslim edilmiştir'), you are usually legally expected to paint it before returning it.

question: Who pays for the annual Kombi (boiler) maintenance?
answer: The tenant is responsible for paying for routine annual maintenance (kombi bakımı). However, if the boiler breaks down due to old age or a part needs replacing, the landlord pays.

question: A pipe burst in the wall. Who pays for the repair?
answer: Structural issues and plumbing problems inside the walls belong to the property. The landlord is responsible for paying the plumber for these major repairs.

question: Who do I talk to if there is a problem with the building?
answer: You should speak to the 'Yönetici' (Building Manager). Their contact information is usually posted on the notice board near the building entrance.

question: Can I rent an apartment via Airbnb for a few months?
answer: Yes, but due to new regulations, short-term rentals under 100 days require special tourism licenses from the Ministry of Culture and Tourism (https://ktb.gov.tr/). Ensure the host is legally licensed.

question: Where can I find flatmates or shared rooms?
answer: Facebook groups like 'Flatshares in Istanbul', university bulletin boards, and websites like Craigslist are popular for finding flatmates and renting a single room.

question: Which banks are the most foreigner-friendly in Turkey?
answer: Ziraat Bankası, İş Bankası, Garanti BBVA, DenizBank, and Kuveyt Türk are known to be more accommodating to foreigners and often have English-speaking staff and English mobile apps.

question: Do I absolutely need a residence permit to open a bank account?
answer: Not always. Some banks (like Ziraat or Kuveyt Türk) allow tourists to open accounts using just their passport, a Turkish Tax Number, and proof of their overseas address, though it depends on the branch manager's discretion.

question: What is a 'Vergi Numarası' and why do banks ask for it?
answer: It is a Turkish Tax Identification Number. Banks are legally required to register this number for every customer to track financial transactions and tax liabilities. You can get it online at: https://ivd.gib.gov.tr/

question: Do banks require a minimum deposit to open an account for foreigners?
answer: Many branches do. If you don't have a residence permit, they might ask you to deposit and block (bloke) a certain amount, such as 10,000 TRY or 1,000 USD, for a few months.

question: Can I open accounts in different currencies?
answer: Yes. When you open a bank account, you can usually open sub-accounts for Turkish Lira (TRY), US Dollars (USD), Euros (EUR), and even Gold (Altın) within the same mobile app.

question: How long does it take to get a debit card?
answer: Some banks will print and give you an unembossed (no name printed) debit card instantly at the branch. If you want a personalized card, it takes about 1-2 weeks to arrive by courier.

question: Can a foreigner get a credit card in Turkey?
answer: Yes, but without a long credit history in Turkey, banks usually require you to deposit a cash block. For example, to get a credit card with a 10,000 TRY limit, you must block 10,000 TRY or 12,000 TRY in your savings account.

question: What is the difference between Havale and EFT?
answer: Havale is a money transfer between two accounts within the SAME bank (instant and usually free). EFT is a transfer between DIFFERENT banks (historically only worked during business hours and had a fee).

question: What is the FAST system?
answer: FAST (Fonların Anlık ve Sürekli Transferi) is an upgraded system launched by the Central Bank that allows you to send money between different banks 24/7 instantly. Learn more: https://www.tcmb.gov.tr/

question: What is an IBAN in Turkey?
answer: The Turkish IBAN starts with 'TR' followed by 24 digits. You must use this complete 26-character alphanumeric code for all domestic wire transfers.

question: Can I receive SWIFT transfers from abroad to my Turkish account?
answer: Yes, you can receive SWIFT transfers by providing your sender with your Turkish IBAN and your bank's SWIFT/BIC code. However, it can take 2-5 business days and incurs high intermediary fees.

question: Are there cheaper alternatives to SWIFT for sending money to Turkey?
answer: Yes, many foreigners use international money transfer services like Wise, Remitly, or Western Union, which are much faster and have lower fees and better exchange rates than traditional SWIFT.

question: Can I use my foreign bank card at Turkish ATMs?
answer: Yes, Visa and Mastercard are accepted at almost all ATMs. However, the Turkish bank AND your home bank will likely charge withdrawal fees and currency conversion fees.

question: Can I withdraw USD or EUR from an ATM in Turkey?
answer: Yes, many ATMs, especially in tourist areas and airports (like TEB, Garanti, İş Bankası), dispense USD, EUR, and sometimes GBP. They usually have clear signs indicating which currencies they hold.

question: What should I do if a Turkish ATM swallows my card?
answer: Immediately call the customer service number written on the ATM. Do not leave the ATM while calling. If the branch is open, go inside and inform the staff immediately.

question: Where is the best place to exchange foreign currency?
answer: Exchange offices (Döviz Bürosu) typically offer better rates than banks and airports. Areas like the Grand Bazaar (Kapalıçarşı) in Istanbul are famous for having the most competitive exchange rates.

question: When is the best time to exchange money on a mobile banking app?
answer: You should only exchange currency on your mobile app during Turkish market hours (Monday to Friday, 09:00 - 17:00). Outside these hours, banks widen the spread (makas), meaning you will lose money on the rate.

question: What is a 'Vadeli Hesap'?
answer: A Vadeli Hesap is a time deposit (savings) account. You lock your money in the account for a specific period (e.g., 32 days, 90 days) to earn interest.

question: Are bank deposits guaranteed by the state?
answer: Yes, the Savings Deposit Insurance Fund (TMSF) insures individual bank deposits up to a specific limit (currently over 600,000 TRY) in case the bank goes bankrupt. Verify limits at: https://www.tmsf.org.tr/

question: What is an 'Otomatik Ödeme Talimatı'?
answer: It is an automatic payment order. You can link your utility bills (water, electricity, internet) to your credit card or bank account so they are paid automatically every month.

question: How can I pay my residence permit tax via mobile banking?
answer: Under the 'Payments' (Ödemeler) section of your app, look for 'Taxes' (Vergiler) and then 'Göç İdaresi İkamet Tezkeresi Harcı'. You will need your application number or tax number.

question: What is a 'Katılım Bankası'?
answer: A Katılım Bankası is an Islamic participation bank (e.g., Kuveyt Türk, Albaraka, Ziraat Katılım). They operate on interest-free banking principles, offering profit-sharing instead of fixed interest.

question: What is Findeks?
answer: Findeks is Turkey's national credit rating system. It tracks your borrowing and repayment history. You need a good Findeks score to get unsecured credit cards or loans. Register at: https://www.findeks.com/

question: How can a foreigner build a credit score in Turkey?
answer: The easiest way is to get a blocked-deposit credit card, use it for daily purchases, and pay the statement balance in full and on time every month. After 6-12 months, your Findeks score will establish.

question: Can foreigners get a mortgage (Konut Kredisi) in Turkey?
answer: Yes, but it is difficult. You typically need a valid residence/work permit, a high official income in Turkey, a good credit score, and banks usually finance only 50-70% of the property's appraised value.

question: What is TROY?
answer: TROY is Turkey's domestic card payment scheme, similar to Visa or Mastercard. Many banks now offer TROY-branded credit and debit cards, which work seamlessly within Turkey.

question: Can I buy Turkish stocks using my bank app?
answer: Yes, if you have a residence permit and a tax number, you can open an investment account (Yatırım Hesabı) through your bank to buy and sell stocks on Borsa Istanbul (BIST).

question: What are standard bank working hours?
answer: Banks are generally open from Monday to Friday, between 09:00 and 17:00. Most branches close for a lunch break between 12:30 and 13:30.

question: Are banks open on weekends in Turkey?
answer: No, standard bank branches are closed on Saturdays and Sundays. Only some special branches in large airports or specific shopping malls might be open.

question: What happens if I enter my ATM PIN wrong 3 times?
answer: For security reasons, your card will be temporarily blocked. You must call customer service or visit a branch with your passport to unblock it and set a new PIN.

question: How do I report a lost or stolen bank card?
answer: Call your bank's customer service immediately. They have 24/7 hotlines, usually with an English option. You can also temporarily freeze your card directly from your mobile app.

question: Is contactless payment widely used in Turkey?
answer: Yes, 'Temassız Ödeme' is extremely popular. Almost all grocery stores, cafes, and taxis accept contactless payments via card, Apple Pay, or Google Wallet.

question: Can I use Apple Pay in Turkey?
answer: Turkish banks do not officially support adding their cards to Apple Pay. However, if you have a foreign card (like a US or UK card) linked to Apple Pay, you can use it on payment terminals in Turkey.

question: What is a 'Sanal Kart' (Virtual Card)?
answer: A Sanal Kart is a digital credit card generated in your mobile app with a different card number. You can set its limit to zero when not using it, making it extremely safe for online shopping.

question: Can I pay my rent in cash?
answer: By law, monthly rental payments above a certain minimum threshold must be paid through banks or the postal system (PTT) for tax tracking purposes. Cash payments are not legally recognized.

question: What is a 'Maaş Müşterisi'?
answer: If your employer deposits your salary into a specific bank, you become a 'Salary Customer'. This usually waives your EFT/Havale transfer fees and offers better loan interest rates.

question: Do Turkish banks charge monthly maintenance fees?
answer: Most banks no longer charge standard monthly account maintenance fees (hesap işletim ücreti) due to consumer protection laws, but they may charge for specific transactions or credit card annual fees.

question: Can I open a joint account (Ortak Hesap) with my spouse?
answer: Yes, you can open a joint account. Both parties need to be present at the bank branch with their passports and tax numbers to sign the agreement.

question: How can I check the daily exchange rates of the Central Bank?
answer: You can check the official daily exchange rates directly on the Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey (TCMB) website: https://www.tcmb.gov.tr/

question: Why did the bank block my account suddenly?
answer: Banks may freeze accounts for suspicious activities, large unexplained inbound international transfers (due to Anti-Money Laundering laws), or if the tax office places a lien (haciz) for unpaid government debts/fines.

question: How do I close my bank account?
answer: If you are leaving Turkey permanently, you must empty the account balance to zero and visit the branch where you opened it to sign account closure documents. It cannot usually be closed via the app.

question: What happens to my bank account if my residence permit expires?
answer: Your account will remain active, but the bank may restrict certain functionalities (like updating your address or applying for new credit) until you provide a renewed residence permit.

question: Is it safe to use public Wi-Fi for mobile banking in Turkey?
answer: No. It is highly recommended to use your mobile data (3G/4G/5G) or a trusted VPN when accessing your bank account to prevent data theft on public Wi-Fi networks.

question: What are common banking scams in Turkey?
answer: A common scam involves receiving SMS messages claiming your account is restricted or you won a prize, with a fake link resembling the bank's login page. Never click these links; always use the official app.

question: Can a bank employee ask for my mobile app password?
answer: Never. Bank employees, police, and prosecutors will never ask for your mobile banking password, PIN, or the SMS OTP code. If someone asks for it, it is a scam.

question: Can I transfer money using only a phone number?
answer: Yes, through the Kolay Adres (Easy Address) system, you can link your mobile phone number, email, or TC/Foreigner ID number to your IBAN, allowing people to send you money using just your phone number.

question: Are there student bank accounts?
answer: Yes, university students under 25 can open 'Genç' or student accounts, which usually have zero transaction fees and offer low-limit, deposit-free credit cards.

question: What is the daily ATM withdrawal limit?
answer: Each bank sets its own daily withdrawal limit, usually ranging from 5,000 to 10,000 TRY. You can withdraw more, but the bank will charge a commission on the excess amount.

question: Can I deposit cash through an ATM?
answer: Yes, most modern ATMs in Turkey accept cash deposits in TRY, and some also accept USD and EUR. The deposited money is instantly credited to your account.

question: How do I update my registered phone number with the bank?
answer: If you change your Turkish phone number, you cannot update it via the mobile app for security reasons. You must visit an ATM with your physical card or go to a branch.

question: What is an Istanbulkart?
answer: Istanbulkart is a reloadable smart card used to pay for all public transportation in Istanbul. Official portal: https://www.istanbulkart.istanbul/

question: Where can I buy an Istanbulkart?
answer: You can buy an anonymous Istanbulkart from the yellow or blue vending machines (Biletmatik) located at almost every metro, tram, and ferry station, or from authorized kiosks.

question: How do I top up my Istanbulkart?
answer: You can add credit using cash at Biletmatik machines, or use a credit/debit card via the official 'Istanbulkart' mobile app.

question: Can multiple people use the same Istanbulkart?
answer: Yes, an anonymous (red) Istanbulkart can be swiped multiple times for different people traveling together. However, discounted personalized cards cannot be shared.

question: What is a personalized Istanbulkart?
answer: A personalized card has your photo and name on it. It allows you to get transfer discounts and is required if you want a monthly subscription (Mavi Kart). Apply via: https://bireysel.istanbulkart.istanbul/

question: How do I apply for a student Istanbulkart?
answer: If you are officially registered at a Turkish university or school, you can apply online or at an Istanbulkart Application Center with your student certificate and passport/kimlik to get heavily discounted fares.

question: What is a 'Mavi Kart' (Blue Card)?
answer: A Mavi Kart is a monthly subscription loaded onto a personalized Istanbulkart. It gives you a set number of trips (usually 200) for a fixed price, valid for 30 days.

question: What is the Metrobüs?
answer: The Metrobüs is a rapid transit bus system in Istanbul that runs on its own dedicated lanes, bypassing traffic. It operates 24/7. Info at: https://iett.istanbul/

question: Why do people tap their card again when leaving the Metrobüs?
answer: Metrobüs fares are distance-based. You pay the maximum fare when entering, and if you travel fewer stops, you tap your card at a refund machine (İade Makinesi) on the way out to get money back.

question: What is Marmaray?
answer: Marmaray is an underground commuter rail line that runs beneath the Bosphorus Strait, connecting the European and Asian continents in minutes. Like the Metrobüs, it uses a distance-based fare system. Official site: https://marmaray.gov.tr/

question: Can I use my credit card to pay for public transport?
answer: Yes, many cities in Turkey now allow contactless credit or debit cards on buses and metros, though it may charge a slightly higher fee compared to using the city's specific transport card.

question: What are the operating hours of the Istanbul Metro?
answer: Most metro lines operate from 06:00 AM to midnight. On weekends (Friday night to Sunday morning), several key metro lines run 24 hours a day.

question: Are there night buses in Istanbul?
answer: Yes, there are special night bus routes (usually starting with the letter 'E' or '15F') that operate after midnight, but they charge a double fare.

question: What is a Dolmuş or Minibüs?
answer: A Dolmuş is a shared taxi or minibus that runs on a set route but without strict stops. You pay the driver in cash depending on how far you are going.

question: How do I know where a Dolmuş is going?
answer: The start and end points, as well as major stops along the route, are displayed on a sign in the front window of the vehicle.

question: How do I tell the Dolmuş driver to stop?
answer: You can politely say 'İnecek var' (Someone will get off) or 'Müsait bir yerde' (At an available spot) when you are close to your destination.

question: Does Uber work in Turkey?
answer: Yes, Uber operates in Turkey, but it functions as an app to hail official yellow, turquoise, or black commercial taxis, rather than private everyday drivers.

question: What are the alternatives to Uber in Turkey?
answer: BiTaksi and iTaksi are the most popular and reliable local apps for hailing official taxis. You can see the estimated fare and pay via credit card through the apps.

question: How does the taxi meter work?
answer: By law, all taxis must use a digital meter (taksimetre) integrated into the rearview mirror. It starts with a base fare and adds up per kilometer. There is also a minimum fare (İndi-Bindi) for short distances.

question: Do taxis accept credit cards?
answer: Many taxis do have POS machines, but they often prefer cash. It is highly recommended to ask 'Kredi kartı geçiyor mu?' (Do you take credit cards?) before getting in.

question: Who pays the bridge or highway toll in a taxi?
answer: If your route crosses a toll bridge, highway, or tunnel (like the Eurasia Tunnel), the toll fee is legally added to the final meter price, and the passenger pays it.

question: How long can I drive in Turkey with a foreign license?
answer: You can drive for up to 6 months from the date you entered Turkey. After 6 months, you must either convert it to a Turkish license or exit and re-enter the country to reset the clock.

question: How do I convert my foreign driving license to a Turkish one?
answer: You need to book an appointment at the Population Directorate (Nüfus: https://randevu.nvi.gov.tr/), provide a notarized translation of your license, a health report, a criminal record check, and pay the license fee.

question: What is the speed limit in Turkey?
answer: Generally, it is 50 km/h in urban areas, 90 km/h on two-lane intercity roads, 110 km/h on divided highways, and 130-140 km/h on motorways (Otoyol), unless signs indicate otherwise.

question: What is HGS?
answer: HGS (Hızlı Geçiş Sistemi) is an electronic toll collection system. You stick an RFID tag on your windshield, and tolls are automatically deducted when you pass through highway and bridge toll booths. Info: https://hgsmusteri.ptt.gov.tr/

question: Where can I buy an HGS sticker?
answer: You can buy it at PTT (post office) branches, banks, or gas stations along the highways. You need your car's registration document (Ruhsat) and your passport/kimlik.

question: What happens if I pass an HGS toll without enough credit?
answer: You have 15 days to top up your HGS account. If you do, the standard toll will be deducted. If you fail to pay within 15 days, you will face a fine up to 4 times the toll amount.

question: Can I rent a car as a foreigner?
answer: Yes, you can easily rent a car. You typically need your passport, a valid driving license (held for at least 1-2 years), and a credit card in your name for the security deposit.

question: What is the minimum age to rent a car in Turkey?
answer: The minimum age is generally 21 for economy cars, but it can be 25 or 27 for luxury or SUV vehicles depending on the rental company.

question: Is car insurance mandatory?
answer: Yes, standard traffic insurance (Trafik Sigortası) is mandatory for all vehicles. When renting, basic insurance is included, but full Casco (Kasko) coverage is recommended.

question: How do I buy a second-hand car?
answer: You find a car, take it to an independent auto-appraisal center (Oto Ekspertiz), and then both buyer and seller go to a Notary to officially transfer the ownership and title deed (Ruhsat).

question: What is a 'Misafir Araç Plakası' (Guest License Plate)?
answer: Foreigners holding a work or study permit can buy tax-free cars from other foreigners or bring their own car. These cars are given a special license plate starting with 'M' (e.g., 34 MA 123).

question: What is the alcohol limit for driving in Turkey?
answer: The legal blood alcohol limit is 0.05% for regular drivers. For commercial drivers (taxis, trucks) and drivers with trailers, the limit is strictly 0.00%.

question: What should I do if I get into a car accident?
answer: If there are no injuries, do not move the cars immediately. Take photos of the scene, then move vehicles to the side. Both drivers must fill out an accident report form (Kaza Tespit Tutanağı). If there is an injury, call the traffic police (112).

question: What is the YHT?
answer: YHT stands for Yüksek Hızlı Tren (High-Speed Train). It is a fast, comfortable, and affordable rail network connecting major cities like Istanbul, Ankara, Eskişehir, and Konya.

question: How do I buy train tickets?
answer: You can buy tickets via the official Turkish State Railways website (https://ebilet.tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr), their mobile app, or directly at the train stations.

question: Do intercity buses have toilets inside?
answer: Most intercity buses in Turkey do NOT have toilets on board. However, they stop at rest areas (Dinlenme Tesisi) every 3-4 hours where you can use facilities and buy food.

question: What is 'Obilet'?
answer: Obilet is the most popular Turkish website and app for comparing prices and buying tickets for intercity buses, flights, and ferries.

question: How do I get to Istanbul Airport (IST)?
answer: You can take the Havaist airport shuttle buses (https://www.hava.ist/) from various points in the city, the M11 Metro line, or a taxi. Travel time depends heavily on traffic but usually takes 45-60 minutes.

question: How do I get to Sabiha Gökçen Airport (SAW)?
answer: You can use the Havabüs shuttles (https://www.havabus.com/) from Taksim or Kadıköy, the M4 Metro line, or take a taxi. It is located on the Asian side of Istanbul.

question: What is 'Martı'?
answer: Martı is the most popular electric scooter sharing app in Turkey. You unlock the scooters on the street using the app and pay per minute. Other apps include BinBin and Hop.

question: Can I ride an electric scooter on the sidewalk?
answer: No. By law, electric scooters must be ridden on bicycle lanes if available, or on the right side of the road. Riding them on pedestrian sidewalks is illegal and subject to fines.

question: Are ferries considered public transport?
answer: Yes, the City Lines ferries (Şehir Hatları) in cities like Istanbul and Izmir are part of the public transport network. You use your Istanbulkart to board them.

question: Can I take my bicycle on public transport?
answer: Yes, bicycles are generally allowed on ferries at all times. On metros and buses, they are only allowed outside of peak rush hours to avoid overcrowding.

question: What is an IDO ferry?
answer: IDO (Istanbul Sea Buses) operates fast catamaran ferries connecting Istanbul to neighboring coastal cities like Bursa and Yalova. They require separate tickets, check: https://www.ido.com.tr/

question: Can I travel intercity with a pet?
answer: Yes, but rules vary. On trains and planes, pets usually need a reservation and a vet health certificate. On buses, small pets in carriers are allowed, but some companies restrict them.

question: Are child car seats mandatory in Turkey?
answer: Yes, by law, children under 150 cm tall and weighing less than 36 kg must use an appropriate child car seat when traveling in a private vehicle.

question: Is hitchhiking safe or common in Turkey?
answer: Hitchhiking (Otostop) is relatively common among university students in Turkey. It is generally safe, especially in coastal and student-heavy areas, but standard precautions should always be taken.

question: How do I report a bad taxi driver?
answer: Note the taxi's license plate number and call the Alo 153 White Desk (Beyaz Masa) hotline or use the app (https://alo153.ibb.gov.tr/). They investigate complaints regarding overcharging, refusing short distances, or rude behavior.

question: Can I walk across the Bosphorus bridges?
answer: No, pedestrians and bicycles are strictly prohibited from crossing the main suspension bridges over the Bosphorus. You must use a vehicle, bus, Metrobus, or ferry to cross continents.

question: What is e-Devlet?
answer: e-Devlet (e-Government) is a centralized online portal providing access to hundreds of Turkish government services, including tax payments, address registration, criminal records, and utility subscriptions. Portal: https://www.turkiye.gov.tr/

question: Can foreigners use the e-Devlet system?
answer: Yes. Once you have a valid Foreigner ID Number (starting with 99) and a registered Turkish phone number, you can get access to the e-Devlet system at https://www.turkiye.gov.tr/

question: How do I get my e-Devlet password?
answer: You must visit any PTT (National Post Office) branch in person with your passport, residence permit card, and Turkish mobile phone. You pay a small fee to receive your initial password via SMS. Find branches: https://www.ptt.gov.tr/

question: Is it safe to drink tap water in Turkey?
answer: In major cities like Istanbul, Ankara, and Izmir, tap water is heavily chlorinated and generally safe for brushing teeth or boiling, but most locals and expats prefer to buy bottled water for drinking.

question: How do I order drinking water for my home?
answer: You can order 19-liter water jugs (Damacana) using mobile apps like GetirSu, Yemeksepeti Su, or BiSU. They deliver it directly to your door within minutes.

question: What are the most popular apps for food delivery?
answer: Yemeksepeti (https://www.yemeksepeti.com/), GetirYemek, and Trendyol Yemek are the most widely used apps for ordering food from local restaurants and international chains.

question: Where can I shop online for everyday items and electronics?
answer: Trendyol (https://www.trendyol.com/), Hepsiburada, and Amazon Turkey are the top e-commerce platforms. They offer fast shipping and a wide variety of products.

question: Do I need to tip in restaurants in Turkey?
answer: Tipping (Bahşiş) is customary. If a service charge is not included in the bill, leaving a 10% to 15% tip in cash is standard for good service in sit-down restaurants.

question: Is bargaining acceptable in Turkey?
answer: Bargaining is common in bazaars (like the Grand Bazaar), antique shops, and when buying real estate or cars. However, it is not acceptable in supermarkets, malls, restaurants, or chain stores.

question: Why do Turks offer tea to guests?
answer: Offering Turkish tea (Çay) is a core symbol of hospitality and friendship. Whether you are visiting a home, a shop, or a bank manager, accepting a glass of tea is a polite way to build rapport.

question: What is Kolonya?
answer: Kolonya (Cologne) is a traditional scented, alcohol-based liquid. It is frequently offered to guests, after meals, or during bus rides as a gesture of hygiene and refreshment.

question: Do I have to take my shoes off when entering a Turkish home?
answer: Yes, taking your shoes off before or immediately upon entering a house is a strict cultural rule. Hosts will usually offer you a pair of indoor slippers (Terlik).

question: How do Turkish people greet each other?
answer: Handshakes are common for formal meetings. Among friends and family, it is customary to kiss on both cheeks. If you are greeting an elderly person, you might see locals kiss their hand and touch it to their forehead as a sign of deep respect.

question: What is the attitude towards street cats and dogs?
answer: Turkey is famous for its love of street animals, especially in Istanbul. Locals feed them, municipalities tag and vaccinate dogs, and it is considered very rude or illegal to harm them.

question: How do I throw away my daily trash?
answer: You usually tie your trash bags and place them in the large communal municipality bins (Çöp Kutusu) located on the street corner. Many neighborhoods have specific collection hours.

question: Is there a recycling system?
answer: Yes, municipalities provide separate bins for paper, plastic, glass, and metal, often located near the regular trash bins. However, the system's efficiency varies by district.

question: What is the emergency number in Turkey?
answer: 112 is the unified national emergency number. You can call it for police, ambulance, fire department, and coast guard services. Info: https://112.gov.tr/

question: How can I report a power outage?
answer: You can call 186, which is the national electricity fault hotline, or check your local electricity distribution company's website (e.g., BEDAŞ in Istanbul: https://www.bedas.com.tr/).

question: What number do I call for a gas leak?
answer: Immediately call 187, the natural gas emergency hotline, and open your windows. Do not use electrical switches or lighters.

question: How do I get home internet installed?
answer: You can apply to providers like Turk Telekom (https://bireysel.turktelekom.com.tr/), Superonline, or Turknet. You will need your passport, residence permit, and exact address.

question: Do government offices speak English?
answer: Generally, no. Except for specific departments in the migration office, most civil servants in tax offices, notaries, and registries do not speak English. It is highly advised to bring a Turkish-speaking friend or translator.

question: What are the working hours of public offices?
answer: Most government offices operate from Monday to Friday, 08:30 to 17:00, with a strict lunch break between 12:30 and 13:30. They are closed on weekends.

question: Are pharmacies open on weekends?
answer: Regular pharmacies (Eczane) are open Monday to Saturday, usually until 19:00, and are closed on Sundays. However, there is always a 'Nöbetçi Eczane' (duty pharmacy) open 24/7 in every district.

question: How do I find a 'Nöbetçi Eczane' at night?
answer: Every closed pharmacy will hang a list or an electronic screen on their door showing the nearest duty pharmacy. You can also check e-Devlet or local pharmacist chamber websites.

question: What is a 'Muhtar'?
answer: A Muhtar is the elected neighborhood headman. They handle local administrative tasks and historically issued proof of address documents, though most of this is now done via e-Devlet.

question: Can I use my foreign electronics in Turkey?
answer: Turkey uses the standard European Type C and Type F (Schuko) plugs with a 220V supply. If your devices use 110V (like in the US), you will need a voltage converter, not just an adapter.

question: What happens if I miss a package delivery?
answer: Cargo companies (Yurtiçi, Aras, MNG, PTT) will usually leave a note on your door. You must go to the local branch indicated on the note with your ID within 3 days to pick it up, or it will be returned to the sender.

question: Can I buy products from Amazon US or AliExpress to Turkey?
answer: Yes, but be aware of strict customs rules. Packages from abroad are subject to high import taxes, and there is a monthly limit on how many international packages you can receive.

question: What measurement system does Turkey use?
answer: Turkey uses the Metric system (kilometers, kilograms, Celsius, liters). Dates are formatted as Day/Month/Year.

question: Are restaurants closed during Ramadan?
answer: In major cities and tourist areas, most restaurants remain open and serve food normally during the day. However, in more conservative neighborhoods, some eateries may close until Iftar (sunset meal).

question: Do shops close during Friday prayers?
answer: Large supermarkets, malls, and chain stores stay open. However, small local businesses and craftsmen might close their shops for about an hour during Friday noon prayers (Cuma Namazı).

question: Can I drink alcohol in public spaces?
answer: While it is legal to buy and consume alcohol in licensed establishments and private homes, drinking openly in parks or on the street is technically prohibited by local municipality regulations and frowned upon.

question: What is the dress code in Turkey?
answer: Turkey is a secular country. In major cities, you can wear western-style clothing. However, modest dress is required when visiting mosques (shoulders and knees covered, headscarf for women).

question: What is CİMER?
answer: CİMER is the Presidential Communication Center. It is a powerful online tool to submit formal complaints, requests, or report illegal activities directly to the state: https://www.cimer.gov.tr/

question: Can foreigners use CİMER?
answer: Yes, foreigners with a valid Foreigner ID Number can log into the CİMER system via e-Devlet and submit petitions.

question: Where can I learn Turkish for free?
answer: Municipalities offer free language courses. In Istanbul, the ISMEK program provides free Turkish lessons. Public Education Centers (Halk Eğitim Merkezi) also offer courses.

question: What is TÖMER?
answer: TÖMER is the Turkish and Foreign Language Research and Application Center. It is the gold standard for learning Turkish and obtaining official language certificates.

question: Are movies in cinemas dubbed or subtitled?
answer: Most adult international movies are shown in their original language with Turkish subtitles (Altyazılı). However, animated films and family movies are usually dubbed in Turkish (Dublajlı).

question: What is a 'Müze Kart' (Museum Pass)?
answer: It is a card granting free or discounted access to hundreds of museums governed by the Ministry of Culture. Official site: https://muze.gov.tr/

question: Can foreigners buy a Müze Kart?
answer: Yes, foreigners holding a valid residence permit (İkamet) can buy the standard citizen Müze Kart at a very low price. Tourists must buy the more expensive 'Museum Pass Turkey'.

question: How do I make a noise complaint against a neighbor?
answer: If polite communication fails, you can call the municipal police (Zabıta) via 153 for daytime noise, or the regular police via 112 for nighttime disturbances.

question: Can I bring my pet on intercity buses?
answer: According to the Ministry of Transport, pets under 8 kg in a locked carrier are legally allowed to travel inside the passenger cabin on intercity buses, provided they don't disturb others.

question: Are veterinary clinics open 24/7?
answer: Many large veterinary clinics and animal hospitals in cities operate 24/7 for emergencies. Routine checkups require appointments during normal hours.

question: How do I sign up for a gym?
answer: You can walk into any private gym with your passport/kimlik. Municipalities also offer very cheap or free sports facilities for residents.

question: What is 'Pazar'?
answer: Pazar is the weekly open-air neighborhood street market. They sell fresh, cheap, and organic fruits, vegetables, cheeses, and sometimes clothing. Each neighborhood has a specific day.

question: Are notary offices open on weekends?
answer: Usually no, but the government introduced a 'Nöbetçi Noter' (Duty Notary) system where a few selected notary offices in each city are open on Saturdays and Sundays. Check: https://portal.tnb.org.tr/

question: Can I use foreign currency for daily shopping?
answer: In highly tourist-heavy areas (like Sultanahmet or Grand Bazaar), some shops accept USD or EUR, but the exchange rate will be poor. Everywhere else, you must use Turkish Lira.

question: What are the major public holidays?
answer: Major secular holidays include Republic Day (Oct 29), Youth and Sports Day (May 19), and Victory Day (Aug 30). Government offices and banks are closed on these days.

question: What is 'Bayram'?
answer: Bayram refers to the major religious holidays: Ramazan Bayramı (Eid al-Fitr, 3 days) and Kurban Bayramı (Eid al-Adha, 4 days). The dates change every year based on the lunar calendar.

question: What happens during Kurban Bayramı?
answer: It is the Feast of Sacrifice. Many businesses close for the entire week. Traditionally, livestock is sacrificed and the meat is shared with family, neighbors, and the poor.

question: How can I make friends as a foreigner in Turkey?
answer: Joining Facebook expat groups, attending language exchange meetups, participating in sports clubs, and using platforms like Internations or Meetup.com are the best ways to build a social circle.

question: What are the most popular expat groups on Facebook?
answer: Groups like 'Expats in Turkey', 'Foreigners in Istanbul', and neighborhood-specific groups are highly active for networking, asking advice, and buying/selling items.

question: Are there English-speaking social clubs?
answer: Yes, major cities have English-speaking Toastmasters clubs, book clubs, and international rotary clubs that welcome foreigners and locals alike.

question: How do I find a language exchange partner?
answer: You can find partners through apps like Tandem and HelloTalk, or by attending weekly 'Spoken Cafe' or language exchange events often hosted in Kadıköy or Beşiktaş.

question: Is Tinder widely used in Turkey?
answer: Yes, Tinder is the most popular dating app in Turkey, widely used by both locals and expats in urban areas.

question: What other dating apps are popular?
answer: Bumble and OkCupid are also very popular, especially among young professionals and expats looking for more detailed profiles.

question: How conservative is the dating culture?
answer: It varies massively by region. In coastal cities and central Istanbul districts, dating culture is very westernized. In more rural or conservative areas, traditional courtship and family involvement are more common.

question: Are there LGBTQ+ friendly spaces in Turkey?
answer: Yes, neighborhoods like Beyoğlu and Kadıköy in Istanbul have established LGBTQ+ friendly cafes, bars, and clubs. However, public displays of affection may draw unwanted attention in conservative areas.

question: What is a 'Meyhane'?
answer: A Meyhane is a traditional Turkish tavern. People go there not just to drink, but to eat meze (tapas), drink Rakı, listen to classical Turkish music, and have deep, long conversations.

question: How are you supposed to drink Rakı?
answer: Rakı is usually mixed with cold water and ice, turning it milky white. You toast by clinking the bottoms of the glasses together. It is meant to be sipped slowly while eating meze and seafood.

question: Is there a strong cafe culture?
answer: Absolutely. Turks spend hours in cafes. You will find everything from traditional tea gardens (Çay Bahçesi) to modern third-wave coffee shops offering artisan espressos and free Wi-Fi.

question: What is 'Nargile'?
answer: Nargile (Hookah or Shisha) is a water pipe used to smoke flavored tobacco. Smoking nargile in a lounge while drinking tea and playing backgammon is a popular social activity.

question: Can I smoke indoors in cafes and bars?
answer: No, smoking indoors is strictly illegal in all public spaces, including restaurants and clubs. However, most venues have covered, heated outdoor terraces where smoking is permitted.

question: Where can I buy tickets for concerts and festivals?
answer: Passo (https://www.passo.com.tr/), Biletix (https://www.biletix.com/), and Bubilet are the primary ticketing platforms for local and international concerts, theater plays, and music festivals.

question: Are there English stand-up comedy shows?
answer: Yes, Istanbul has a growing English comedy scene. Venues like The Clap or BKM Mutfak occasionally host English open-mic nights and touring international comedians.

question: How can I volunteer in Turkey?
answer: Foreigners can volunteer with local NGOs, animal shelters, or organizations like TEGV (Education Volunteers). However, formal long-term volunteering may require specific visa statuses.

question: Where can I find software development or tech meetups?
answer: Platforms like Meetup.com and Kommunity are excellent for finding local developer groups, coding workshops, and tech networking events in major cities, particularly around Levent and Kadıköy.

question: Are there coworking spaces for digital nomads?
answer: Yes, spaces like Kolektif House, Workinton, and Impact Hub offer excellent facilities, fast internet, and vibrant communities for freelancers and digital nomads.

question: What is the most popular sport in Turkey?
answer: Football (Soccer) is a massive cultural phenomenon. The rivalry between the 'Big Three' Istanbul clubs (Galatasaray, Fenerbahçe, Beşiktaş) dominates sports culture.

question: How can I buy tickets for a football match?
answer: You must first apply for a 'Passolig' card online. Once approved, you can buy electronic match tickets which are loaded directly onto your Passolig card. Portal: https://www.passolig.com.tr/

question: Are there amateur sports leagues I can join?
answer: Yes, many expats and locals organize amateur football (halı saha), basketball, and volleyball games. Facebook groups and local sports complexes are the best places to find teams.

question: Where can I run or cycle safely in Istanbul?
answer: The Caddebostan-Moda coastal path on the Asian side, the Bebek-Sarıyer coastline on the European side, and the Belgrad Forest are the safest and most popular areas for running and cycling.

question: How do Turkish people celebrate birthdays?
answer: Birthdays are usually celebrated by gathering at a cafe or restaurant with a cake. It is customary for the person whose birthday it is to NOT pay the bill; their friends cover it.

question: What should I bring when invited to a Turkish home?
answer: It is polite to bring dessert (like Baklava or chocolates) or something for the house. Never bring alcohol unless you are absolutely certain the hosts drink.

question: Are there cooking classes for foreigners to learn Turkish cuisine, like making traditional sherbet or desserts?
answer: Yes, institutions like the Culinary Arts Academy (MSA) and various boutique workshops offer English-taught classes on classic Turkish cooking, Ottoman desserts, and traditional drinks like sherbet (şerbet).

question: Is it common to split the bill at restaurants?
answer: Yes, among friends it is very common to split the bill equally or pay for what you ordered. In Turkey, this is colloquially called 'Alman Usulü' (The German Way).

question: Who pays on a date?
answer: Traditionally, the man is expected to pay on the first date. However, in modern, younger circles, offering to split the bill is becoming more common and appreciated.

question: Are there international churches or places of worship?
answer: Yes, major cities have Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant churches, as well as Synagogues. You can attend services in English, French, Italian, and other languages.

question: Are there English-speaking AA (Alcoholics Anonymous) meetings?
answer: Yes, there are active English-speaking AA and NA groups in Istanbul, usually holding meetings in neighborhoods like Beyoğlu or Şişli.

question: How do I find expat families with kids?
answer: International schools and local 'Expat Moms/Parents' Facebook groups are the best hubs for arranging playdates and finding family-friendly events.

question: Are there international schools for children?
answer: Yes, there are excellent international schools offering IB, British, or American curricula (e.g., MEF, IICS, British International School). Be aware that tuition fees are generally high.

question: What is the nightlife like in Istanbul?
answer: It is highly vibrant. Kadıköy offers relaxed pubs and rock bars, Karaköy has trendy cocktail lounges, and areas like Ortaköy and Beşiktaş host high-end nightclubs.

question: Are nightclubs strict with entry?
answer: Very. Most popular nightclubs enforce a 'Damsız Girilmez' rule, meaning groups of single men will not be allowed in. You generally need a balanced male-to-female ratio.

question: Is it safe for women to go out at night?
answer: In central, busy areas like Kadıköy, Beşiktaş, and Nişantaşı, it is generally safe. However, standard city precautions apply: avoid poorly lit streets and taking unlicensed taxis alone late at night.

question: What are the best neighborhoods for expats to live in?
answer: On the European side: Cihangir, Nişantaşı, Beşiktaş, and Sarıyer. On the Asian side: Moda, Suadiye, and Caddebostan are highly favored by expats.

question: Are there board game cafes in Turkey?
answer: Yes, board game cafes (Kutu Oyunu Kafe) are extremely popular among Turkish youth. They offer hundreds of games and usually charge a small hourly cover fee per person.

question: Is it easy to find English books?
answer: Yes, large bookstores like D&R or Pandora carry English sections. You can also order a vast selection online through Amazon Turkey or local site Idefix.

question: What is NadirKitap?
answer: NadirKitap is an incredible online marketplace for second-hand and antique books. You can find out-of-print English books sold by local 'Sahaf' (second-hand booksellers). Site: https://www.nadirkitap.com/

question: Are there public libraries I can use?
answer: Yes, municipalities operate public libraries. You can use their study spaces for free, and if you have a residence permit, you can register to borrow books.

question: What are traditional Turkish arts I can learn as a hobby?
answer: Many expats enjoy taking workshops in Ebru (paper marbling), Hat (Islamic calligraphy), or Çini (traditional ceramic tile painting).

question: Can foreigners join public choirs or theater groups?
answer: Yes, if your Turkish is good enough, you can join municipal choirs or amateur theater groups. There are also a few English-speaking amateur acting troupes.

question: How do I connect with my home country's community?
answer: Many consulates organize cultural events, national day celebrations, or have affiliated cultural centers (like the British Council, Goethe-Institut, or Institut Français) that act as community hubs.

question: Is it common to have house parties?
answer: Yes, but noise complaints are common. Apartment walls are often thin, and Turkish neighbors will not hesitate to call the police if the music is too loud after midnight.

question: What is 'Okey' and why is everyone playing it?
answer: Okey is a traditional tile-based game, similar to Rummikub. It is the most popular game played in cafes alongside backgammon (Tavla).

question: Do I need to tip taxi drivers?
answer: No, tipping taxi drivers is not expected. However, people usually round up the fare to the nearest whole bill for convenience (e.g., paying 100 TRY for a 95 TRY fare).

question: How do Turks view personal space?
answer: Turks have a smaller bubble of personal space compared to Northern Europeans or Americans. Standing close in lines or touching arms during conversation is completely normal.

question: What does 'Geçmiş Olsun' mean?
answer: It translates to 'May it pass' or 'Get well soon'. You say this to someone who is sick, who has just gone through a stressful event, or even when someone's favorite sports team loses.

question: What does 'Kolay Gelsin' mean?
answer: It translates to 'May it be easy for you'. It is a polite greeting used when approaching anyone who is working, from a shop clerk to a street cleaner.

question: Is it okay to talk about politics?
answer: Turks love discussing politics, but as a foreigner, it is best to listen rather than heavily critique. Political polarization is high, and certain topics require sensitivity.

question: Can I drink the water from public street fountains?
answer: Many historic mosques and streets have stone fountains (Çeşme). Unless locals are drinking from it, it's safer to assume the water is for washing, not drinking.

question: Can I apply for a work permit myself?
answer: No. In Turkey, the employer must apply for your work permit (Çalışma İzni) on your behalf through the Ministry of Labor and Social Security. Portal: https://ecalismaizni.csgb.gov.tr/

question: What is the 5-to-1 rule for hiring foreigners?
answer: To hire one foreign employee, a Turkish company must generally employ at least five Turkish citizens. Exceptions exist for foreign direct investments, certain tourism sectors, and domestic workers.

question: Do I need a residence permit to get a work permit?
answer: If you are applying from within Turkey, you must have a valid residence permit with at least 6 months of validity remaining. Otherwise, you must apply from the Turkish consulate in your home country.

question: Does a work permit replace a residence permit?
answer: Yes. A valid work permit automatically acts as a residence permit for the duration of its validity. You do not need to hold both simultaneously.

question: What happens to my work permit if I quit my job?
answer: Your work permit is tied to your specific employer. If you resign or are fired, your work permit is canceled. You usually have 10 days to leave the country or apply for a residence permit.

question: Can a foreign student work full-time?
answer: No. Associate and undergraduate students cannot work full-time. They can only work part-time (up to 30-35 hours/week) after their first year. Master's and PhD students have more flexible rights.

question: What is the Independent Work Permit (Bağımsız Çalışma İzni)?
answer: It is granted to foreigners who have lived in Turkey legally and continuously for at least 5 years and whose professional activities will create significant economic or employment value.

question: Can foreigners work as doctors or nurses in Turkey?
answer: Yes, but it is heavily regulated. Foreign healthcare professionals must pass an equivalency exam, get approval from the Ministry of Health, and speak Turkish at least at a B2 level.

question: Are there jobs foreigners are legally banned from doing?
answer: Yes. Foreigners cannot work as lawyers, notaries, judges, dentists, veterinarians, pharmacists, customs brokers, or tourist guides in Turkey.

question: What is an exceptionally qualified workforce (Turquoise Card)?
answer: The Turquoise Card (Turkuaz Kart) is similar to a US Green Card. It is given to highly skilled foreigners, investors, scientists, and artists, granting an indefinite right to work and reside.

question: What are the best job portals in Turkey?
answer: LinkedIn is best for corporate jobs. Kariyer.net (https://www.kariyer.net/) is the largest local portal. Youthall is great for students, and Secretcv is another popular alternative.

question: Do I need a Turkish-style CV to apply for jobs?
answer: Most Turkish companies expect a standard CV (Özgeçmiş) that includes a professional headshot photo, your date of birth, marital status, and a clear career timeline.

question: Is knowing Turkish mandatory to find a job?
answer: For local companies, yes. However, in IT, international sales, tourism, and multinational corporations (like Unilever or gaming startups), fluent English is often sufficient.

question: How long does a hiring process usually take?
answer: Corporate hiring can be slow, often taking 3 to 6 weeks. It usually involves HR screening, a technical or case study interview, and a final interview with the department head.

question: What is the minimum wage for foreigners?
answer: By law, the minimum wage for a foreign employee cannot be lower than the national minimum wage. For highly skilled roles, the Ministry requires the salary to be 3 to 6 times the minimum wage.

question: What does 'Net Salary' vs 'Gross Salary' mean in Turkey?
answer: Net salary is what hits your bank account. Gross salary includes your net pay plus SGK (social security) premiums, income tax, and stamp tax. Always negotiate your salary in 'Net' terms.

question: Do companies pay for my daily lunch?
answer: Yes, providing a meal allowance is standard practice. Companies either have a cafeteria (Yemekhane) or provide a food card (like Multinet, Ticket, or Sodexo) loaded with a daily allowance.

question: What is a 'Yol Parası' (Commute Allowance)?
answer: Employers typically cover your daily commuting costs. They either provide a company shuttle bus (Servis) or add a monthly public transport allowance to your salary.

question: How many hours is the standard workweek?
answer: The legal maximum standard workweek is 45 hours. In corporate offices, it is typically Monday to Friday, 09:00 to 18:00, including a one-hour lunch break.

question: Do I get paid for overtime?
answer: Yes. If you work more than 45 hours a week, you are legally entitled to overtime pay at 1.5 times your regular hourly rate, or equivalent time off.

question: How many days of paid vacation do I get?
answer: After completing one full year at a company, you earn 14 working days of paid annual leave. This increases to 20 days after 5 years, and 26 days after 15 years.

question: What happens on public holidays?
answer: Employees are legally entitled to full pay on national and religious holidays without working. If you are required to work on a public holiday, you must receive double pay for that day.

question: Can I be fired without a reason?
answer: During the initial 2-month probation period (Deneme Süresi), either party can terminate the contract without notice. After that, the employer must provide a valid, documented reason and give formal notice.

question: What is severance pay (Kıdem Tazminatı)?
answer: If you work at a company for more than one year and are fired without just cause (or retire/leave for mandatory military service), you are entitled to severance pay equal to one gross month's salary for every year worked.

question: What is notice pay (İhbar Tazminatı)?
answer: If an employer fires you without giving the legally required notice period (which ranges from 2 to 8 weeks depending on tenure), they must pay you the salary for that period upfront.

question: How much notice do I have to give when resigning?
answer: The legal notice period depends on how long you've worked there: 2 weeks (0-6 months tenure), 4 weeks (6-18 months), 6 weeks (18-36 months), or 8 weeks (3+ years).

question: Can a foreigner start a company in Turkey?
answer: Yes, a foreigner can establish a Limited Liability Company (Limited Şirket) or a Joint Stock Company (Anonim Şirket) with 100% foreign ownership. A local partner is not legally required.

question: Do I need a work permit if I own a company?
answer: Owning shares in a company does not automatically give you a work permit. If you want to act as the company director/manager and work actively, you must apply for a work permit.

question: What is a 'Mali Müşavir'?
answer: A Mali Müşavir is a Certified Public Accountant. If you set up a company in Turkey, you are legally required to hire an independent Mali Müşavir to handle your monthly tax declarations and bookkeeping.

question: What is a 'Şahıs Şirketi' (Sole Proprietorship)?
answer: It is the simplest company structure, ideal for freelancers. However, as a foreigner, setting up a Şahıs Şirketi is very difficult unless you already have a 5-year independent work permit or are married to a Turkish citizen.

question: Can I work remotely for a foreign company while living in Turkey?
answer: Yes. Many digital nomads work for foreign companies while residing in Turkey on a tourist residence permit. However, technically, a tourist permit does not authorize active employment, so you cannot take local Turkish clients.

question: Do I pay taxes in Turkey if I work remotely for a US company?
answer: If you live in Turkey for more than 183 days a year, you are considered a tax resident. By law, you must declare and pay income tax on your global income, though double-taxation treaties may apply.

question: Is coworking culture popular?
answer: Very popular. Istanbul has dozens of modern coworking spaces offering flexible hot-desking, private offices, and networking events for freelancers and remote workers.

question: What is the dress code in Turkish offices?
answer: Banks and law firms require formal business attire (suits). Tech companies, startups, and creative agencies are very casual. Most corporate offices lean towards 'business casual'.

question: How do colleagues address each other?
answer: It is very common to use first names followed by 'Bey' (Mr.) or 'Hanım' (Ms.)—for example, 'Ahmet Bey' or 'Ayşe Hanım'. Calling a manager just by their first name is considered rude unless instructed otherwise.

question: Is the workplace hierarchy strict?
